titleStmt.title: ‘In my beginning is my end’: Facilitating Open Scholarship and Reusability across the European Research Area

author.affiliation: DARIAH and Belgrade Center for Digital Humanities
DARIAH and Ghent Centre for Digital Humanities
DARIAH and Digital Curation Unit, R.C. "Athena"

keywords.term: digital ecologies and digital communities creation management and analysis
digital research infrastructures development and analysis
I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

p.hi: When creating digital humanities resources –archives, projects, datasets, and multimedia– researchers have typically focused on three key questions: what technologies to use, who the audience will be, and who to involve in the creation process (Purcell, 2019; Hayler, 2016;
Warwick, 2012

). However, over the past decade, questions around the creation of digital scholarship have been slowly shifting. While the creation of digital archives centred around a particular individual or theme are still an important part of digital scholarship in the humanities and datasets are developed to answer specific research questions (McGillivray, et al., 2020), increasingly we are asking about solutions for long-term sustainability beyond the hope that our institutions will keep the project going (
Fenlon, 2020, Smithies et al., 2019).

As Mary Queen of Scots is reputed to have embroidered onto a pillow in the years prior to her death, ‘In my end is my beginning’, or as TS Eliot reframed this in his late poem, ‘Four Quartets’: ‘In my beginning is my end’, in the digital humanities community we have come to realise that decisions made at the inception of our digital projects play an outsized role in their afterlife. While, thankfully, the vast majority of us do not need to think about our imminent demise at the end of a literal chopping block, the metaphorical one looms large for the digital scholarship we create.

The funding mechanisms we live and work within (Otis, 2023), as well as our systems of academic rewards (Hazelkorn, 2015), favours discreet scholarship with a clear beginning, middle, and end, typically culminating in a tangible product that can be made available as a type of online publication (e.g. it will remain on an institutional server for
N
number of years): a product with a web presence that can be pointed to and advertised by university marketing (
Edmond and Morselli 2020)

. This product-focused approach neglects the broader spectrum of valuable contributions made throughout the research process (Tasovac et al. 2023). This is why increasingly there is recognition that workflows, methodologies, and intermediate outputs are equally deserving of preservation and recognition: “it is not just the product, however incomplete, that [should be] seen as worthy of preservation for current and future generations, but also equally the process (or indeed processes) for producing it” (Viola 2023: 59).

To foster this shift in perspective and ensure that it gains wide acceptance in the research community, our funders and our institutions must realise that we are no longer operating in static, linear environments but within digital ecosystems that should prioritize both use and reuse. We are not calling for the abolition of traditional digital archives, boutique digital collections, or the individually-curated dataset. Such projects remain key to many disciplinary fields in the humanities and to researchers' own specialisations. What we are arguing for instead is the need for a fundamental change in mindset in order to design and develop digital artefacts in such a way that they integrate seamlessly into broader ecosystems of FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) data, services, and workflows for use and reuse (Baillot et al., 2016; Tóth-Czifra & Romary, 2020; Wilkinson, et al., 2016).

As individuals, our ability to preserve our digital scholarship is inherently limited. Yet, within new research infrastructures, digital scholarship has the possibility of living on beyond the purpose for which it was originally created. Digital Sustainability is a topic that is receiving an increasing amount of attention within the DH community (e.g. Barats, 2020;
Cummings, 2020; Holmes, et al, 2023; Godard, 2019; Miller, 2022).
Institutional, national, or community-based repositories serve as key mechanisms for the archiving and long-term preservation of digital artifacts (
Anglada, et al., 2023;
Pennock, 2024;
Shearer, et al., 2023
). Here data can be extracted from websites into datasets, enriched with metadata, and stored. Even entire data-driven websites can be preserved (
Milligan, Ruest, & Lin, 2016; Schafer & Winters, 2021; Vlassenroot, et al., 2019)

in formats like the ISO WARC standard (BnF). Yet, even if a project team plans for sustainability and data is deposited in a repository, there is no guarantee that it finds an audience, or that even if found, the audience knows how to use it.

The paper will explore the roles that Research Infrastructures (RIs) should play in facilitating the “digital afterlife” of DH projects. We define the “digital afterlife” as the period during which DH projects as complex research outputs continue to generate scholarly, societal or cultural value after direct funding or active development come to an end. In this context, one of the most pressing questions is: what should be archived, and for how long? Should everything be preserved for the long-term, as is the case with scholarly articles in journals anticipating that scholarship will, ultimately, find its audience? Or should we take an archival approach in which the data is curated and retained (The National Archives, [n.d.]; Sabharwal, 2015; Spek & Links, 2013) based on expert judgement. These questions underscore the importance of taking a systematic approach, as has been happening across the European Research Area with the creation of research infrastructures (OECD 2023; Ribeiro 2021; Bolliger and Griffiths 2020; Edmond et al. 2020).

Research infrastructures in the Social Sciences and Humanities, such as DARIAH, CLARIN and CESSDA (ESFRI 2024), represent a significant commitment from European Member States and the EU, with substantial funds allocated to their development, maintenance, and long-term sustainability. These infrastructures offer transnational mechanisms for addressing the challenges of digital sustainability. With the increasing emphasis on Open Research, driven through initiatives such as the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC), the emphasis on scholarly reuse is only going to grow in the future. In our paper, we identify and analyze three key areas in which digital research infrastructures should play an important role in enabling and supporting the “digital afterlife” of DH projects: conceptual frameworks, technological solutions and strategic policy work.

item.hi: Conceptual frameworks: shifting the narrative from product to process.

Historically, DH projects have often been understood primarily as products—self-contained digital archives, datasets, or multimedia outputs. However, this product-focused perspective overlooks the iterative, collaborative, and experimental nature of much of DH work. To address this, we argue for a conceptual shift that positions process as a central element of both scholarly evaluation

preservation. This reframing recognizes that the workflows, methodologies, and intermediate outputs of DH projects are in some instances as valuable as the final product, often providing insights into the evolution of the research. Platforms such as the SSH Open Marketplace (Barbot et al. 2024) provide a useful framework for sharing and citing (DARIAH, 2019) these processes as first-class research outputs.
Technological solutions: designing for ecosystems of use and reuse.

To support the “digital afterlife” of DH projects, it is crucial to adopt technological practices that facilitate interoperability, adaptability, and long-term sustainability from the inception of each and every project. This approach involves adopting open standards (JSON, XML, SQL, Unicode etc.); implementing modular design principles for datasets and tools; ensuring resources are accessible via APIs; providing comprehensive technical documentation; and avoiding the creation of bespoke platforms that rely on ad hoc integrations of various software packages, which makes them difficult to maintain or adapt once the original development team moves on
Strategic policy work: championing sustainable DH.

To fully realize the potential of DH projects in the “digital afterlife,” digital research infrastructures, within the broader context of Open Science, should actively work to reshape the funding, evaluation, and governance models that underpin DH work, emphasizing sustainability, interoperability, and long-term impact. Key strategies include advocating for funding models that prioritize long-term sustainability, encouraging the co-creation of best practices for designing interoperable digital projects, leveraging existing RIs in the SSH domain such as DARIAH, CLARIN and the broader initiatives in cultural heritage such as European Collaborative Cloud for Cultural Heritage (ECCCH) and the Common European Data Space for Cultural Heritage to establish networks of digital scholarship that transcend institutional and disciplinary silos), while developing clear guidelines for determining which data should be preserved, for how long, and under what conditions. This involves both proactive curation strategies and mechanisms for retiring datasets that no longer serve a meaningful purpose.

p.hi: We argue that the successful digital afterlife of DH projects will to a large extent depend on a conceptual shift in how we conceive, design, implement and evaluate digital research. By embedding sustainability, interoperability and reuse into the very fabric of DH practices – and the design of DH projects from their inception – we should be in a much better position
to ensure that the scholarly, societal and cultural value of digital projects continues long after their original funding and development have ended. Although this paper

focuses on the potential of European research infrastructures to transform how we sustain and engage with DH scholarship, the model we describe may prove valuable to other countries and/or regions. However, as institutions and national initiatives decide to tackle this ever-growing issue, we are confident that the solutions can be found through the combined efforts of conceptual rethinking, technological solutions and strategic advocacy.

bibl.hi: Anglada, Lluís, David Antos, Pierre-Yves Burgi, Cécile Cavet, Draženko Celjak, Ingrid Dillo, Olesea Dubois, et al. ‘How a European Network of Fair-enabling Trustworthy Data Repositories Can Align to the Vision of EOSC’. Zenodo, 25 January 2023.
Baillot, Anne, Mike Mertens, and Laurent Romary. "Data fluidity in DARIAH–pushing the agenda forward." Bibliothek Forschung und Praxis 40, no. 2 (2016): 151-165.
Barats, Christine, Valérie Schafer, and Andreas Fickers. 2020. "Fading Away... The Challenge of Sustainability in Digital Studies."
DHQ: Digital Humanities Quarterly
Barbot, L., M. Dolinar, E. J. Gray, C. Grisot, K. Illmayer, M. Kurzmeier, and B. McGillivray. 2024. "Contextualizing Research Tools and Services Through Workflows in the SSH Open Marketplace."
Journal of Open Humanities Data
BnF. "The WARC File Format (ISO 28500) - Information, Maintenance, Drafts." Date Accessed 8.12.2024.
Bolliger, Isabel K., and Alexandra Griffiths. 2020. “The Introduction of Esfri and the Rise of National Research Infrastructure Roadmaps in Europe.” In
Big Science and Research Infrastructures in Europe
, edited by Katharina C. Cramer and Olof Hallonsten, 101–27. Cheltenham, UK: Edward Elgar Publishing.
Callon, Michel. 1994. "Is Science a Public Good? Fifth Mullins Lecture, Virginia Polytechnic Institute, 23 March 1993."
Science, Technology, & Human Values
Cummings, Rebekah, David S. Roh, and Elizabeth Callaway. 2020. "Organic and Locally Sourced: Growing a Digital Humanities Lab with an Eye Towards Sustainability."
DHQ: Digital Humanities Quarterly
DARIAH Winter School: Open Data Citation for Social Science and Humanities (2019).

Edmond, Jennifer, Toma Tasovac, Frank Fisher, and Laurent Romary. 2020. “Springing the Floor for a Different Kind of Dance: Building Dariah as a Twenty-First-century Research Infrastructure for the Arts and Humanities.” In
Digital Technology and the Practices of Humanities Research
, edited by Jennifer Edmond, 207–34. Cambridge, UK: Open Book Publishers.
Edmond, Jennifer, and Francesca Morselli. 2020. "Sustainability of Digital Humanities Projects as a Publication and Documentation Challenge."
Journal of Documentation
ESFRI. 2024. “Social Sciences and Humanities.” In
ESFRI Landscape Analysis 2024
Fenlon, Katrina Simone. 2020. "Sustaining Digital Humanities Collections: Challenges and Community-Centred Strategies."
International Journal of Digital Curation
Goddard, Lisa, and Dean Seeman. 2019. "Negotiating Sustainability: Building Digital Humanities Projects That Last." In
Doing More Digital Humanities
, 38–57. Routledge.
Hallonsten, Olof. 2020. "The Role of Research Infrastructures in Innovation Systems: The Case of Swedish Participation in the Halden Reactor Project (HRP)." In
Big Science and Research Infrastructures in Europe
, edited by Katharina C. Cramer and Olof Hallonsten. Cheltenham, UK: Edward Elgar Publishing.
Hayler, Matt, ed. 2016.
Research Methods for Creating and Curating Data in the Digital Humanities
. Edinburgh: Edinburgh University Press.
Hazelkorn, Ellen. "Making an impact: New directions for arts and humanities research." Arts and Humanities in Higher Education 14, no. 1 (2015): 25-44.
Holmes, Martin, Janelle Jenstad, and J. Matthew Huculak. "Introduction to Special Issue: Project Resiliency in the Digital Humanities." DHQ: Digital Humanities Quarterly 17, no. 1 (2023).
McGillivray, B., Poibeau, T., & Ruiz Fabo, P. (2020). Digital Humanities and Natural Language Processing:“Je t’aime... Moi non plus”. DHQ, Digital Humanities Quarterly. 2020, 14.2
Miller, A., and Molly Taylor-Poleskey. 2020. "Digital Humanities Preservation: A Conversation for Developing Sustainable Digital Projects." In
Transformative Digital Humanities
, 103–19. Routledge.

Milligan, Ian, Nick Ruest, and Jimmy Lin. "Content selection and curation for web archiving: The gatekeepers vs. the masses." In Proceedings of the 16th ACM/IEEE-CS on Joint Conference on Digital Libraries, pp. 107-110. 2016.
OECD. 2023. “Very Large Research Infrastructures: Policy Issues and Options.”
OECD Science, Technology and Industry Policy Papers
Otis, Jessica. 2023. "Follow the Money?: Funding and Digital Sustainability."
DHQ: Digital Humanities Quarterly
Pennock, Maureen. "Disentangling Digital Preservation Risk: An Interdisciplinary Exploration and Solution." (2024). Doctoral Thesis. Dundee.
Purcell, Aaron D., ed. 2019.
The Digital Archives Handbook: A Guide to Creation, Management, and Preservation
. Lanham, MD: Rowman & Littlefield.
Ribeiro, Margarida. 2021. “Towards a Sustainable European Research Infrastructures Ecosystem.” In
The Economics of Big Science
, edited by Hans Peter Beck and Panagiotis Charitos, 7–13. Cham: Springer International Publishing.
Sabharwal, Arjun. Digital curation in the digital humanities: Preserving and promoting archival and special collections. Chandos Publishing, 2015.
Schafer, Valérie, and Jane Winters. "The values of web archives." International Journal of Digital Humanities 2, no. 1 (2021): 129-144.

Shearer, Kathleen, Silvia Mirlene Nakano Koga, Eloy Rodrigues, Natalia Manola, martine Pronk, and Vanessa Proudman. ‘Current State and Future Directions for Open Repositories in Europe’. Zenodo, 5 December 2023.

Digital Humanities Quarterly
Speck, Reto, and Petra Links. "The missing voice: archivists and infrastructures for humanities research." International Journal of Humanities and Arts Computing 7, no. 1-2 (2013): 128-146

The Role of Research Infrastructures in the Research Assessment Reform: A DARIAH Position Paper.

The National Archives. [n.d.] Guidance for Retention:
Viola, Lorella. 2023.
The Humanities in the Digital: Beyond Critical Digital Humanities.
Cham: Palgrave Macmillan.

Digital Humanities in Practice.
London: Facet Publishing.
